skill.name: lay-summary-gen
skill.description: Converts complex medical abstracts into plain language summaries for patients, the public, and media audiences.
skill.summary: Converts complex medical abstracts into plain language summaries for patients, the public, and media audiences.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged 40 patterns across 4 files but all are false positives after semantic evaluation. The 26 external_commands matches are markdown code blocks in SKILL.md documenting example usage, not actual backtick execution. The 2 network matches are a GitHub source attribution URL. The 2 filesystem matches are security checklist items documenting path traversal prevention. The 10 blocker patterns (weak cryptographic algorithm, system reconnaissance) are all text pattern false positives on documentation and audit JSON content. The Python script (scripts/main.py) is a legitimate text processing tool with no network access, no file system writes, and no malicious behavior. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or credential theft detected. The only real risk is that the skill executes a packaged Python script, which is expected for this type of tool.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Code Blocks Flagged as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged 26 occurrences of backtick characters in SKILL.md as shell/backtick execution. All occurrences are within markdown code fences (```bash) documenting example commands for users. No actual backtick execution exists - these are documentation examples, not executable code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Source Attribution URL Flagged as Network Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged a GitHub URL on SKILL.md line 7 as hardcoded network access. The URL is a source attribution link in a markdown blockquote pointing to the skill's own repository. No actual network requests are made by the skill code - this is purely informational documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Security Checklist Items Flagged as Path Traversal
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged SKILL.md lines 126 and 129 as path traversal sequences. These lines are security checklist items that explicitly document prevention: 'No unauthorized file system access (../)' and 'Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'. These are protective measures being documented, not path traversal attacks.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Text Flagged as Weak Cryptography
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged multiple locations in SKILL.md and the audit JSON file as containing weak cryptographic algorithms. All flagged locations are plain documentation text (YAML frontmatter, markdown tables, evaluation notes) that coincidentally matched pattern signatures. No cryptographic code exists in any file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Audit Evaluation Text Flagged as System Reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged locations in SKILL.md and the audit JSON file as system reconnaissance. All flagged locations are evaluation notes, test case descriptions, and assertion documentation - none contain any reconnaissance or information gathering logic.

content.user_title: Generate Plain-Language Medical Summaries with Claude Code
content.value_statement: Complex medical research is hard for non-experts to understand. This skill converts medical abstracts into clear, simple language that patients and the public can read.
actual_capabilities: Convert medical abstracts into plain language summaries at a Grade 6-8 reading level
Replace complex medical jargon with simple, understandable terms using a built-in dictionary
Extract up to three key takeaways from research papers for non-expert audiences
Estimate reading grade level of generated summaries based on word complexity
Support EU Clinical Trials Regulation lay summary formatting requirements
Execute as a standalone Python script with command-line or programmatic interfaces
limitations: Cannot verify the medical accuracy or factual correctness of the original abstract content
Jargon replacement is limited to an 11-term predefined dictionary and may miss specialized terminology
Reading level estimation uses a simple word-length heuristic rather than formal readability formulas like Flesch-Kincaid
Requires Python 3.10 or higher to run the packaged script locally

use_cases.title: Clinical Research Communication
use_cases.description: A clinical researcher converts trial results into plain language summaries for patient portals, public disclosure, and regulatory compliance.

use_cases.title: Patient Education Materials
use_cases.description: A health educator transforms medical journal findings into easy-to-read handouts for patients and caregivers.

use_cases.title: Science Journalism
use_cases.description: A science writer quickly generates simplified summaries of medical papers to inform accessible news articles and press releases.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Medical Abstract Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize this medical abstract in simple language that anyone can understand: [paste your abstract here]
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick plain-language conversion of a single abstract

prompt_templates.title: Patient-Facing Lay Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this clinical trial abstract into a plain language summary for patients. Keep it under 250 words and replace all medical jargon: [paste your abstract here]
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating patient education content with word limit

prompt_templates.title: Public Audience Summary with Takeaways
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a lay summary of this medical research for a general public audience. Replace all jargon with simple terms, target a Grade 6-8 reading level, and extract the 3 most important takeaways: [paste your abstract here]
prompt_templates.scenario: Public health communication with structured output

prompt_templates.title: EU CTR-Compliant Lay Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an EU Clinical Trials Regulation compliant lay summary from this abstract. Target audience: general public. Maximum 250 words. Include: a plain language summary, reading level assessment, 3 key takeaways, and a table of jargon terms that were replaced: [paste your abstract here]
prompt_templates.scenario: Regulatory compliance submission for EU clinical trials

output_examples.input: This randomized controlled trial evaluated the efficacy of Drug X versus placebo in 200 patients with chronic migraine over a 12-week period.
output_examples.output: This study tested how well Drug X works for chronic migraines. Researchers randomly assigned 200 patients to receive either Drug X or an inactive substance. The study lasted 12 weeks and compared results between the two groups.

output_examples.input: A double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter study demonstrated significant reduction in adverse events among patients receiving the experimental therapy compared to standard care.
output_examples.output: A study run at multiple hospitals found fewer side effects with the new treatment. During the study, neither the patients nor the researchers knew who received the new treatment and who received standard care. This helped make the results fair and reliable.

best_practices: Always have a qualified professional review AI-generated medical summaries for accuracy before sharing with patients or the public
Provide the complete abstract text rather than partial excerpts to get the most accurate and complete lay summary
Specify your target audience as patients, public, or media to receive language tailored to the appropriate health literacy level
anti_patterns: Do not use AI-generated lay summaries as a substitute for reading and understanding the full original research paper
Do not assume the built-in jargon dictionary covers all specialized terms, especially in highly technical or niche medical fields
Do not publish or distribute generated summaries without human review, particularly for regulatory submissions or patient-facing materials

faq.question: What types of documents can this skill summarize?
faq.answer: It works best with medical and clinical research abstracts, but can process any scientific abstract that contains technical terminology needing simplification.

faq.question: Does this skill require an internet connection?
faq.answer: No. All text processing happens locally using Python. The skill makes no external API calls and does not send data anywhere.

faq.question: What reading level does the output target?
faq.answer: The default target is Grade 6-8, which aligns with health literacy best practices for communicating with general public audiences.

faq.question: Can I customize the medical jargon dictionary?
faq.answer: Yes. Edit the JARGON dictionary in the scripts/main.py file to add, remove, or modify the medical terms and their plain-language replacements.

faq.question: Is the generated output medically reviewed?
faq.answer: No. The skill performs text simplification only. All outputs should be reviewed by a qualified medical professional before clinical or public use.

faq.question: Does this skill comply with EU Clinical Trials Regulation requirements?
faq.answer: The skill is designed to support EU CTR lay summary formatting requirements, but final regulatory compliance depends on your organization's review and approval process.
